title: Whittled sycamore taper candle holder
subtitle: A seasoned-wood upright holder for a standard taper candle
excerpt: A simple taper candle holder in seasoned sycamore: a cylinder with a tapered socket drilled or whittled to hold a standard 22 mm taper candle. The body is octagonal in section, tapering slightly toward the base. The challenge is cutting the socket cleanly without a lathe; the method uses drill bit and knife to produce a clean, tight fit.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for socket-cutting in treen; traditional candlestick proportions from Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry.

content.text: A taper candle holder is one of the most useful pieces of small treen. It is a single block of seasoned wood with a socket at the top and a finished octagonal body that sits steady on a flat surface. The socket is the technical challenge. It needs to hold a standard 22 mm taper candle firmly, without being so tight that the candle cracks the wood. This tutorial makes the socket with a brace-and-bit drill (optional) and a chip-carving knife. The exterior is then shaped with a sloyd knife.
The wood

A block of kiln-dried sycamore: 8 cm long, 5 cm square. Sycamore is the preferred material for small treen; pale, close-grained, and stable. Beech works equally well and produces a more pronounced grain pattern. Avoid pine and oak: pine is too soft for a clean socket, and oak's tannins can react with candle wax.
Tools
A
sloyd knife
for the main body shaping, a
chip-carving knife

for the socket, and a brace-and-bit with a 20 mm bit if available to rough out the socket hole. Danish oil for finishing. The chip-carving knife is the critical tool: its wide point is what allows clean vertical cuts into the socket without the handle fouling the sides.
Method
Use
push cuts
throughout the exterior shaping. Strop every 15 minutes. Work with the block clamped to the bench for the socket work; the chip-carving knife needs both hands and a stable block.
Mark the socket centre on one end-grain face of the block. The socket should be centred and 2 cm deep. If you have a brace-and-bit, drill a 20 mm hole 1.5 cm deep at the centre mark.

Cut the socket to its final diameter (22 mm) with the chip-carving knife. Hold the knife with thumb on the spine, point down into the hole, and rotate the block against the blade in 5 mm increments. The blade pares the socket wall to a consistent diameter. Test the fit with a spare taper candle every few passes; the candle should seat 1.5 cm deep and feel snug but not tight.

Shape the exterior. Mark the octagonal profile on the end-grain: eight equal sides around the diameter. Work down each face with push cuts along the grain, removing the corners. The body should taper very slightly toward the base; 5 cm square at the top to 4 cm square at the base; which gives the holder visual stability.
Chamfer the bottom edges to create a flat base that will not rock. Four stop cuts at the base corners followed by push cuts to remove the triangular waste.
Sand through 120 and 180 grit on the exterior faces. Leave the socket interior unsanded; a smooth socket grips the candle wax less effectively than a slightly textured one.
Finishing

Apply Danish oil to the exterior faces, let it soak for 15 minutes, wipe off the excess. One coat is sufficient for indoor use. Leave the socket interior unfinished. Danish oil sets hard in 24 hours; the holder is ready to use the following day. Do not use a wax finish on the exterior; the socket rim is close to the candle flame and a wax finish on the holder rim would soften under heat.
Variations
Beech: similar to sycamore, more visible grain. Works identically.
Cluster of three at different heights: make three holders from the same blank, cut to 6 cm, 8 cm, and 10 cm. Group them together.
Pyrography decoration: once the Danish oil has cured for 2 days, add a simple border design at the base before the final oil coat.
Care
Clear candle wax from the socket by warming slightly with a hair dryer and wiping with a cloth. Do not immerse in water. Re-oil the exterior annually.

attrs.intro: Common problems with the candle holder.

items.fix: Enlarge the socket with further chip-carving knife passes. Test with the candle frequently to avoid over-cutting.
items.symptom: Socket too tight; candle splits the holder

items.fix: The socket cannot be made smaller once cut. Line the socket with a wrap of brown paper gummed to the socket wall with a drop of wood glue. The paper provides 1 to 2 mm of padding.
items.symptom: Socket too loose; candle wobbles and falls

items.fix: Use only kiln-dried stock. Check the grain direction before starting; it should run exactly along the length of the holder, not across it.
items.cause: Wood not fully dry or grain running at an angle to the block face

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
